mappings.rationale: Directly covers the nature and scope of the Earth sciences including geology, ocean systems, and Earth's structure.
Section B.2 covers geologic sciences as a core Earth science discipline including petrology, geochemistry, and stratigraphy.
Covers the integrated study of Earth's systems, connecting physical geography (B.1), geologic sciences (B.2), and atmospheric sciences.
Section A.5.a.vi specifically covers plate tectonics as a unification of continental drift and seafloor spreading.
Section A.5.a.iii covers advances in geophysics including seismology and the study of Earth's internal structure.
Sections A.2.a.ii (speculations about fossils), A.3.a.ii (paleontology/stratigraphy), and A.5.a.v (advances in paleontology) all cover fossils.
Section A.4.a.i covers classification of rocks, and A.5.a.ii covers experimental petrology.
Section A.3.a.i covers the beginnings of mineralogy and study of ore deposits, and A.4.a.i covers classification of minerals.
Section A.2.a.i covers speculations about volcanic eruptions, and volcanology is part of the geologic sciences (B.2).
Section A.4.a.iv specifically covers evidence for an Ice Age and the work of Louis Agassiz.
Section A.5.b.v covers the study of glacier motion and high-latitude ice sheets, and B.3 lists glaciology.
Climate change connects to atmospheric sciences (A.5.c) and the classification of climate (A.5.c.v).
Section A.5.c.v covers the classification of climate, and atmospheric sciences are a core Earth science discipline.
Section A.4.c.iii covers the study of weather, and A.5.c.ii covers advances in weather forecasting.
Section A.3.c covers atmospheric sciences from early studies through modern technology, and A.5.c covers 20th-century advances.
Section A.4.b.ii covers the beginnings of oceanography, and A.5.b covers ocean circulation, currents, and ocean floor charting.

mappings.vsiAuthor: David George Bowers, Emyr Martyn Roberts
mappings.rationale: Section A.2.b.iii covers the study of tides as part of hydrologic and atmospheric sciences.
Section A.4.b.i covers the study of groundwater flow and surface water discharge; rivers are central to hydrologic sciences.
Lakes are part of the hydrologic sciences, and B.3 lists limnology as a hydrologic discipline.
Section A.5.b.i covers the study of water resources and seawater chemistry, and water is fundamental to hydrologic sciences.
Section A.4.a.vi covers concepts of landform evolution, and B.2 lists geomorphology as a geologic science.
Deserts represent a geomorphological environment covered under physical geography (B.1) and climate classification.
Mountains are key landforms covered under physical geography (B.1) and relate to plate tectonics and geomorphology.

mappings.vsiAuthor: John A. Matthews, David T. Herbert
mappings.rationale: Section B.1 covers physical geography including distribution and spatial patterns of soils, water, climate, and landforms.
The Antarctic involves multiple Earth sciences including glaciology (A.5.b.v), atmospheric sciences, and ocean sciences.
The Arctic involves multiple Earth sciences including glaciology (A.5.b.v), atmospheric sciences, and ocean sciences.
Covers earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and climate hazards studied through the Earth sciences in sections A and B.
Coral reefs relate to marine geology, oceanography (A.5.b), and paleontology.
Sections A.4.a.ii and A.5.a.v cover paleontological advances including faunal succession and Precambrian life.
Dinosaurs are a major subject of paleontology covered in A.4.a.ii and A.5.a.v.
Mass extinctions are studied through paleontology and stratigraphy in A.4.a.ii and A.5.a.v.
Section A.5.a.i covers the development of radiometric dating, which relies on radioactivity for geological time.
The Anthropocene is a proposed geological epoch connecting geologic and atmospheric sciences in this section.
Biogeography connects physical geography (B.1) with ecology and the distribution of life forms on Earth.
Savannas represent a climatic and biogeographic zone studied through physical geography (B.1) and climate classification.
Marine biology connects to oceanography (B.3), studying life in ocean environments alongside physical ocean sciences.
Section A.5.a.iv covers astrogeologic research applying Earth sciences to planetary investigation.
Navigation historically depended on understanding Earth sciences including geography, atmospheric sciences, and ocean currents.
Section A.2 covers Earth science ideas from antiquity including speculations about earthquakes, fossils, and landforms.
Section A.4.a.ii covers Darwin's concept of faunal succession and organic evolution, linking biological and geological sciences.
